Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 - The Intelligence Cycle and Intelligence-Led
Policing
Intelligence-led policing is the deliberate use of evaluated intelligence to direct resources and operations, rather
than purely reactive response to reported crime. Its engine is the intelligence cycle - a continuous process that
turns raw information into actionable intelligence and feeds the result back into further collection. This chapter
sets out the cycle and the structures that drive it.

1.1 The Intelligence Cycle
The classical intelligence cycle has five stages, operating as a continuous loop rather than a single pass:

1.1.1 Direction
Setting the intelligence requirement - what do we need to know, and why.
Driven by priorities - threat-to-life, organised crime, local crime patterns, specific operations.
Tasking - allocating collection effort to the requirement.
Strategic direction (Divisional / national priorities) and tactical direction (this operation, this target).

1.1.2 Collection
Gathering information from all available source types (Chapters 3-7).
HUMINT - human sources, community, witnesses.
OSINT - open source.
Technical / surveillance.
Financial.
Forensic / database.
Collection must be lawful and proportionate - the power and authorisation framework applies.

1.1.3 Processing / Collation
Recording the information on the intelligence system.
Grading - source and information evaluation (Chapter 2).
Storage with appropriate handling code and access control.
Linking - connecting to existing records (persons, vehicles, locations, events).
This is where information becomes retrievable, comparable intelligence.

1.1.4 Analysis
Turning collated intelligence into meaning.
Identifying patterns, links, series, networks, trends.
Analytical products - target profiles, network charts, crime-pattern analysis, problem profiles.
Filling intelligence gaps - identifying what is still needed (feeding back to Direction).
Assessment - what does this tell us; what is likely; what should we do.

1.1.5 Dissemination
Getting the intelligence to those who need it to act.
Within the handling code - need-to-know; source and method protection.
Briefings, intelligence reports, tasking documents, alerts.
Dissemination generates action, which generates new information - closing the loop.

1.1.6 The Loop and Review
The cycle is continuous. Dissemination and action generate new information that re-enters collection. Each

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

pass should be reviewed - was the intelligence accurate, was the action effective, what gaps remain. Review
prevents the cycle ossifying around assumptions.

1.2 Intelligence-Led Policing - The National Intelligence Model
The National Intelligence Model (NIM) is the business model that structures intelligence-led policing. Its core
features:

1.2.1 The Three Levels
Level

Scope

Example

Level 1 - Local

District / local issues

Local burglary series, street-level drug dealing,
anti-social hotspot

Level 2 - Cross-border /
regional

Issues spanning
Divisions

Level 3 - Serious /
organised

National / international

Districts

/

Travelling crime gang, regional drug supply line
Organised crime group, large-scale trafficking,
terrorism

1.2.2 Tasking and Coordination
Tasking and Coordination Group (TCG) meetings - the decision forum.
Strategic Tasking - sets medium/long-term priorities (the control strategy).
Tactical Tasking - allocates resources to current operations and targets.
Regular cycle - intelligence reviewed, priorities set, resources directed, results assessed.

1.2.3 The Four Analytical Products
Strategic Assessment - the big picture; what are the threats and priorities.
Tactical Assessment - the current operational picture; what needs action now.
Target Profile - everything known about a specific individual / group.
Problem Profile - analysis of a specific crime series / hotspot / issue.

1.2.4 The Four Intelligence Assets
Sources - people and systems that provide information (knowledge assets).
Systems - the databases and tools (PULSE, GISS, analytical software).
People - trained members (analysts, handlers, investigators).
Knowledge products - the assessments and profiles produced.

1.3 The Inner-City Application
In a dense inner-city District, the intelligence cycle runs fast and local. Practical features:
Beat-level collection is continuous - every interaction is potential intelligence.
The local crime office / intelligence unit is the collation hub.
Tasking is often immediate - a fresh report drives a same-shift operation.
Level 1 / Level 2 boundary is fluid - local dealing connects to organised supply.
Community intelligence is rich but must be handled with care for source safety in tight-knit areas.

1.4 From Cycle to Action - The Decision Point
Intelligence exists to support a decision. Common intelligence-driven decisions:
Warrant application - intelligence grounds the sworn information (Chapter 8 on the evidential divide).
Arrest - intelligence informs reasonable suspicion (but the suspicion must be the member's own - Walsh).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Surveillance authorisation - intelligence justifies the necessity and proportionality.
Stop and search - intelligence can form part of reasonable suspicion.
Threat-to-life warning - intelligence triggers the Osman / protective duty.
Resource deployment - intelligence directs patrol and operation.
Disruption - intelligence enables lawful disruption short of prosecution.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 - Grading and Evaluation
Every piece of intelligence must be evaluated before it can be safely used. The grading system separates the
reliability of the source from the validity of the information, and attaches a handling code controlling
dissemination. This discipline prevents the central error of intelligence work: treating an unreliable report as
fact. The system is commonly known by its dimensions - a source-information-handling matrix (historically
'5x5x5', often now operated as '3x5x5').

2.1 Why Separate Source from Information
A reliable source can pass on bad information (they were deceived); an unreliable source can occasionally be
right. Grading the two separately forces the member to think about both: how much do I trust this person, and
how much do I trust this specific claim. Conflating them is how bad intelligence gets acted upon.

2.2 Dimension 1 - Source Evaluation
The source evaluation assesses the reliability of the source of the information. A common five-point scale
(letters A-E or equivalent):
Grade

Meaning

Application

A

Always reliable

Proven, tested source with consistent reliable history; technical
product (e.g. authorised intercept)

B

Mostly reliable

Source reliable in most instances; generally dependable

C

Sometimes reliable

Source reliable on some occasions; mixed history

D

Unreliable

Source has provided unreliable information; reason to doubt

E

Untested

New source with no track record; reliability cannot yet be judged

Note: an untested source (E) is not the same as an unreliable one (D). Untested means unknown, not bad. Much
community intelligence starts at E and earns a better grade over time.

2.3 Dimension 2 - Information / Intelligence Evaluation
The information evaluation assesses the validity of the specific information, independent of the source. A
common five-point scale (1-5):
Grade

Meaning

Application

1

Known to be true without reservation

Witnessed directly by the source; first-hand; verifiable

2

Known personally to source but not to
officer

Source has first-hand
independently confirm

3

Not known personally to source but
corroborated

Source heard it but it is supported by other information

4

Not known
corroborated

Hearsay, uncorroborated; treat with caution

5

Suspected to be false / malicious

personally

and

not

knowledge;

officer

cannot

Reason to believe the information is untrue or maliciously
provided

2.4 Dimension 3 - Handling Code
The handling code controls dissemination - who may receive the intelligence and under what conditions. It
protects sources, methods, and the integrity of operations. A common five-point scale (1-5):

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Code

Meaning

1

May be disseminated within the police service / to other law enforcement (lawful sharing); standard

2

May be disseminated to non-prosecuting bodies / partners, subject to conditions

3

May be disseminated to (non-EU / foreign) bodies, subject to conditions and risk assessment

4

Dissemination only within originating unit / receiving unit subject to specified conditions heightened restriction

5

No further dissemination - refer to originator; highest restriction (e.g. source-protective,
operationally sensitive)

2.5 Putting It Together - A Worked Example (Generic)
Worked example - illustrative only, not a real case
A member receives a report from a community contact that a particular flat is being used to store firearms. The
contact has given accurate information twice before (Source grade B - mostly reliable). The contact says they
personally saw a firearm in the flat (Information grade 2 - first-hand to source). Because disclosure could identify
the source and endanger them, the report is marked Handling code 5 - no further dissemination, refer to originator.
The graded report (B-2-5) is recorded on the intelligence system. It is sufficient to justify development surveillance authorisation, further collection - but on its own it is intelligence, not evidence. To act with a warrant,
the member builds independent corroboration (Chapter 8 sterile corridor) so the warrant rests on lawful,
disclosable grounds.

2.6 The Disciplines of Grading
Grade honestly - the grade reflects genuine reliability, never inflated to justify action.
Grade contemporaneously - at the time of recording, not retrospectively.
Separate the dimensions - think source and information independently.
Record the basis - why this grade; what the source's access is; what corroboration exists.
Review grades - reliability changes; a source that proves wrong is downgraded.
Sanitise where needed - the recorded intelligence can describe the information without revealing the source's
identity.

2.7 Sanitisation
Sanitisation is the removal of source-identifying detail from an intelligence report so it can be disseminated or
developed without compromising the source. Done well, it preserves the operational value while protecting the
person. Example: 'a source states' rather than naming circumstances that would identify which of three people
present could have known. Sanitisation is a skill - it must not so distort the report that the recipient misjudges
its reliability (hence the grade travels with it).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 - HUMINT - Human Intelligence
Human intelligence - information from people - remains the richest and most contextual source. It spans the
casual community contact, the witness, the member's own beat knowledge, and the formally registered Covert
Human Intelligence Source (CHIS). This chapter covers the full spectrum, old craft and modern framework,
with particular attention to the legal and welfare discipline of source handling.

3.1 The Spectrum of Human Sources
Type

Description

Framework

Beat knowledge

The member's own accumulated knowledge of
the area, people, patterns

Professional observation; recorded as
intelligence

Community
contacts

Residents, shopkeepers, community workers
who share information openly

Open contact; record as intelligence;
protect where needed

Witnesses

Persons with direct knowledge of an event

Statement
evidence

One-off tip-offs, reports, Crimestoppers

Record and grade; anonymity often

Registered CHIS

Covert source in a managed, authorised
relationship

Garda CHIS policy - strict regime (3.4)

Prisoners

/

Information from detained persons

Caution re reliability and motive; recorded

/

Banks, social services, other agencies (within
legal gateways)

Statutory / policy information-sharing
gateways

Members
public

of

framework;

may

become

3.2 Beat Knowledge - The Foundation Craft
The oldest and still most valuable intelligence asset is the member who knows their patch. Beat knowledge is
built deliberately:
Presence - being on the ground, visible, consistent, in the same area over time.
Observation - who associates with whom; whose car is whose; what is normal and what is not.
Conversation - routine, non-confrontational engagement with residents, traders, young people.
Recording - beat knowledge that stays in one member's head dies when they transfer. Record it as intelligence
so it becomes a Divisional asset.
The anomaly instinct - beat knowledge makes the abnormal visible: the new face, the unusual gathering, the
car that does not belong.

3.3 Cultivating and Receiving Community Intelligence
Approachability - communities share with members they trust and can reach.
Discretion - a community contact who is seen to be 'talking to the Guards' may be endangered, especially in
tight-knit or intimidated areas.
Reliability assessment - the SOURCE mnemonic; what is their access, motive, history.
Motive awareness - information may be offered to settle a score, divert suspicion, or mislead; assess motive
(the 'Reason' in SOURCE).
Recording - every meaningful report recorded and graded, however informal the encounter.
Protecting the casual source - even an open community contact may need source-protective handling if
disclosure would endanger them.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.4 Covert Human Intelligence Sources (CHIS)
A CHIS is a person who establishes or maintains a relationship for the covert purpose of obtaining and passing
information. CHIS use is one of the most powerful and most tightly regulated intelligence methods. It operates
under the Garda Síochána policy framework on the use of CHIS, with strict registration, handling, authorisation,
and oversight.

3.4.1 Core Principles of CHIS Management
Registration - a CHIS must be formally registered; no 'unofficial' sources.
Two-handler rule - never a single-handler relationship; at least two handlers plus a controller, preventing overfamiliarity, corruption, and dependency.
Authorisation - the use and conduct of a CHIS requires authorisation at the appropriate level, recorded and
reviewed.
Tasking - what the CHIS is asked to do is recorded; the CHIS must not be tasked to act as an agent provocateur
(inciting crime that would not otherwise occur).
Risk assessment - continuous assessment of risk to the source, ongoing throughout the relationship.
Welfare - the source's safety and welfare are a duty of care held by the handlers and the organisation.
Records - dedicated, secure source records, separate from the investigation file (the 'source file').
Need-to-know - the source's identity is restricted to the handling team and protected absolutely outside it.

3.4.2 The Source File / Investigation File Separation
Source records are kept separate from the investigation file.
This protects the source's identity from disclosure in the investigation / prosecution.
Intelligence from the source enters the investigation in sanitised, graded form.
Where the intelligence is to ground action (e.g. a warrant), independent corroboration is built so the source need
not be revealed (sterile corridor, Chapter 8).

3.4.3 The Agent Provocateur Limit
A CHIS (or any source / member) must not incite, instigate, or procure the commission of an offence that the
suspect would not otherwise have committed. Evidence obtained through entrapment of this kind is liable to
exclusion, and the conduct is itself improper. The source may report on, and be present at, planned criminality
- but must not be the cause of it. This is a critical line in tasking.

3.4.4 Reliability and Motive in CHIS
CHIS motives vary - financial reward, reduced exposure on their own matters, grievance, conscience, control.
Motive shapes reliability - a source minimising their own role may shade information.
Corroboration before action - never act on uncorroborated single-source product where the stakes are high.
The handler's judgment - experienced handlers read their sources; this judgment is itself part of the grading.

3.5 Witnesses as Intelligence and Evidence
Witnesses sit at the intelligence-evidence boundary - what they know may be both.
Initial account is intelligence (directs the investigation); the formal statement is evidence.
Reluctant / intimidated witnesses - witness protection considerations; anonymity frameworks where lawful.
Inner-city reality - witness intimidation is a live threat in some communities; intelligence about intimidation is
itself important.

3.6 Information from Persons in Custody
Detained persons may offer information - on their own matter or others'.
Reliability caution - motive to deflect, reduce exposure, or curry favour is high.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Recording - any such information is recorded and graded like any other.
Inducement limit - information must not be obtained by improper inducement that would taint a statement (Vol
2 / Vol 9 custody framework).

3.7 Institutional and Inter-Agency HUMINT
Banks and financial institutions - via the financial intelligence framework (Chapter 6).
Social services / Tusla - within child-protection and statutory gateways.
Other agencies (Revenue, Welfare, Immigration) - within lawful information-sharing gateways.
Foreign law enforcement - via Interpol / Europol / liaison channels.
Each gateway has its legal basis - share within it, document the basis.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 4 - OSINT - Open Source Intelligence
Open Source Intelligence is intelligence derived from publicly available information - social media, websites,
public registers, media, marketplaces. It is the fastest-growing intelligence discipline because so much of
modern life is documented online. It is powerful, cheap, and lawful when done within the open-source boundary
- but that boundary, and the rules on recording and on covert online activity, must be understood.

4.1 What Counts as Open Source
Social media - public posts, profiles, photos, associations, check-ins.
Marketplace and classified sites - vehicles, goods (including stolen property), rentals.
Public registers - Companies Registration Office, property, planning, court lists.
Media - news, local press, death notices (RIP.ie is a notable Irish associational resource).
Websites and forums - public-facing content.
Mapping / imagery - street-level and satellite imagery for area familiarisation.
The open / surface web generally - anything a member of the public can access without deception or special
authority.

4.2 The Open-Source Boundary
OSINT is lawful where the information is genuinely open - accessible to any member of the public without
deception, without circumventing privacy settings, and without establishing a relationship under a false identity.
The line:
Open source (generally lawful)

Beyond open source (needs authority)

Viewing a public social media profile

Accessing a private / restricted profile

Reading public posts

Friending / following under a false identity to gain
access

Searching public registers

Building a covert online persona to engage a target

One-off viewing of public content

Repeated / systematic monitoring amounting to
surveillance

Noting publicly visible associations

Interacting covertly to elicit information (CHIS-like
online)

Crossing the boundary - covert engagement, false personas, accessing private content, or systematic monitoring
that becomes directed surveillance - engages the CHIS framework (Chapter 3) and/or the surveillance
authorisation framework (Chapter 5). Open viewing is one thing; covert online relationship-building is another.

4.3 Repeated Monitoring Becomes Surveillance
A single look at a public profile is open-source research. Systematic, repeated, recorded monitoring of an
individual's online activity over time can amount to directed surveillance, engaging the proportionality and
authorisation considerations of the surveillance framework. The more sustained and targeted the online
observation, the more it requires the discipline (and potentially the authorisation) of surveillance rather than
casual research.

4.4 OSINT Tradecraft
Attribution caution - confirm an account genuinely belongs to the target; impersonation and shared accounts
are common.
Capture properly - screenshot with date/time and URL; content is deleted and edited constantly.
Preserve metadata where lawful and possible - timestamps, account identifiers.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Corroborate - online claims and personas are routinely false; grade accordingly (often information grade 3-4).
Operational security - searches from attributable accounts/devices can alert a target; use appropriate research
facilities, not personal accounts.
Record as intelligence - OSINT findings are graded and recorded like any other source.

4.5 OSINT Applications
Locating persons - missing persons, wanted persons, bail-breakers via public posts/check-ins.
Associations and networks - mapping who connects to whom.
Lifestyle / proceeds indicators - displays of wealth inconsistent with known income (feeds financial
intelligence, Chapter 6).
Stolen property - marketplaces and classifieds.
Event awareness - planned gatherings, feuds, threats made online.
Corroboration - confirming or contradicting other intelligence.
Pre-operational familiarisation - imagery of premises and area.

4.6 Digital Footprint and the Wider Picture
A person's digital footprint extends beyond what they post - it includes what others post about them, their
associations' content, and public records. Building an OSINT picture means triangulating across sources. But
the same cautions intensify: more inference means more risk of error, and the privacy intrusion of building a
comprehensive profile is greater than any single search - proportionality applies to the aggregate, not just each
step.

4.7 The Boundary with Private Data
Private messages, restricted accounts, and content behind authentication are not open source.
Access to such content requires the appropriate legal mechanism - production order, warrant, or mutual legal
assistance for overseas platforms.
Communications content vs communications data - distinct frameworks (Chapter 5).
Never circumvent privacy settings or platform security - that is not OSINT, it is unauthorised access.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 5 - Technical and Surveillance Intelligence
Surveillance and technical methods - watching, following, recording, tracking, and accessing communications
data - are among the most intrusive intelligence techniques and among the most tightly regulated. The Criminal
Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 governs covert surveillance; the Communications (Retention of Data)
framework governs access to communications data; ANPR and CCTV operate within their own frameworks.
This chapter sets out the methods and their legal discipline.

5.1 The Spectrum of Surveillance
Type

Description

Authority

Routine observation

Open observation in public; brief, nonsystematic

General duty; record reason

Directed
surveillance

Covert, systematic observation of specific
persons

Surveillance framework; authorisation

Intrusive / tracking

Surveillance
monitoring

devices,

CJ (Surveillance) Act 2009 - judicial /
senior authorisation

Communications
data

Who contacted whom, when, where (not
content)

Comms (Retention of Data) framework
- post-Dwyer

Interception
(content)

Content of communications

Separate
statutory
framework - exceptional

devices,

tracking

interception

5.2 The Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009
The 2009 Act provides the statutory basis for covert surveillance by Gardai (and other designated agencies) and
for the admissibility of surveillance evidence. Core features:
Authorisation - covert surveillance generally requires authorisation; the standard route is a judicial authorisation
from a District Court judge.
Approval (urgent cases) - a written approval by a senior officer (superintendent or above) in cases of urgency,
for a limited period, with subsequent reporting.
Tracking devices - a distinct, somewhat less onerous approval regime for tracking devices.
Necessity and proportionality - the authorisation must be necessary (for preventing/detecting arrestable
offences, maintaining security of the State, saving life) and proportionate.
Documentation - applications, authorisations, and use are documented and subject to oversight.
Oversight - a designated High Court judge oversees the operation of the Act; a complaints mechanism exists.
Admissibility - evidence obtained under a valid authorisation is admissible; the Act provides the framework
that makes covert surveillance product usable in court.

5.2.1 Necessity and Proportionality in Practice
Necessity - is the surveillance genuinely needed; could the objective be achieved by less intrusive means.
Proportionality - is the intrusion justified by the seriousness of what is being investigated.
Collateral intrusion - what intrusion on third parties (family, neighbours, associates) will occur, and is it
minimised.
These are recorded in the application - the justification must be articulable, not assumed.

5.3 Physical Surveillance Tradecraft (Old Craft, Still Essential)
Foot and mobile surveillance - following without being detected; team discipline.
Static observation posts - watching a location over time.
The log - contemporaneous surveillance log is both intelligence and potential evidence; times, observations,

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

exhibits.
Anti-surveillance awareness - targets, especially organised ones, run counter-surveillance; tradecraft must
account for it.
Authorisation discipline - sustained covert following / watching is directed surveillance and engages the
framework; do not drift into it without authority.

5.4 ANPR - Automatic Number Plate Recognition
Cameras read vehicle registration plates and check against databases.
Real-time alerts - flagged vehicles (no insurance, wanted, intelligence interest).
Historical data - vehicle movement patterns, association of vehicles, journey reconstruction.
Powerful for - locating vehicles, proving movement, linking vehicles to locations/times.
Framework - operated within the data protection and policing-purpose framework; access for a policing
purpose, recorded.
Old-craft parallel - ANPR is the industrialised version of the member noting a registration; it scales but the
interpretation still needs human judgment.

5.5 CCTV
Garda, local authority, transport, and private CCTV networks.
Real-time monitoring (town-centre systems) and retrospective recovery.
Recovery discipline - identify cameras early (overwrite cycles of 7-31 days are common), seize/copy with
continuity (Vol 9 PEMS), statement from system operator.
Intelligence and evidence - CCTV is often both; the intelligence picture (who, where, when) and the evidential
exhibit.
Coverage mapping - in an inner-city District, knowing the camera estate (which premises, which streets) is
itself beat knowledge.

5.6 Communications Data
Communications data - the who/when/where of communications, not the content - is governed by the
Communications (Retention of Data) framework, substantially reshaped by the Dwyer line and the 2022 Act
(treated in detail in Vol 9 Chapter 2). For intelligence purposes:
Subscriber data - who holds a number / account.
Traffic data - patterns of contact between numbers.
Location data - cell-site association placing a phone in an area.
Access requires the proper statutory authority - targeted, necessary, proportionate, authorised (post-Dwyer
regime).
Content is separate - interception of content is an exceptional, separately-governed power.
Cell-site analysis - expert interpretation of which masts a phone used, placing it geographically; expert evidence
in court.

5.7 Phone and Device Forensics
Extraction - trained examiner using approved tools (e.g. Cellebrite-type extraction).
Integrity - hash the device/image before examination; document the process; continuity.
Product - contacts, messages, media, app data, location history - rich intelligence and evidence.
Legal basis - lawful seizure and examination; encryption and password-compulsion limits apply.
Specialist - Garda Cyber Crime Bureau (GCCB) / trained examiners; do not improvise device handling.

5.8 The Surveillance Discipline - Summary

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Before any covert surveillance - PLAN
Power: what authority (2009 Act authorisation/approval, comms data framework, tracking-device approval)?
Lawful authorisation at the correct level before you begin. Articulate necessity and proportionality, including
collateral intrusion. Necessity recorded - the documented justification is what makes the product admissible and
the conduct defensible. Drifting from routine observation into directed/intrusive surveillance without authority
is the central trap - when watching becomes systematic and covert, stop and get the authority.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 6 - Financial Intelligence
Financial intelligence follows the money. Acquisitive and organised crime exists to generate profit; the money
leaves a trail that is often more durable and more revealing than the crime itself. Financial intelligence spans
suspicious transaction reporting, the Financial Intelligence Unit, lifestyle analysis, and the proceeds-of-crime
architecture operated through the Criminal Assets Bureau. 'Follow the money' is old wisdom; the modern
framework industrialises it.

6.1 The Financial Intelligence Architecture
Element
Financial
Ireland)

Function
Intelligence

Unit

(FIU

Receives and analyses Suspicious Transaction Reports; central
financial intelligence hub

Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs)

Mandatory reports by banks / designated persons of suspicious activity

Criminal Assets Bureau (CAB)

Multi-agency body targeting proceeds of crime (civil and criminal)

Garda National Economic
Bureau (GNECB)
Money laundering framework

Crime

Fraud, money laundering, complex financial crime investigation
Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010

6.2 Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs)
Banks, financial institutions, and 'designated persons' (accountants, solicitors, estate agents, etc.) are legally
required to report suspicious transactions.
STRs flow to the FIU (and Revenue).
They are a major intelligence stream - flagging unexplained wealth, structuring, suspicious movement.
For the operational member - awareness that financial institutions are a reporting partner; intelligence may
originate here.

6.3 Lifestyle and Proceeds Analysis
The core question - does the person's lifestyle exceed their known legitimate income.
Indicators - property, vehicles, travel, spending inconsistent with declared/known income.
Sources - OSINT (visible wealth, Chapter 4), surveillance, financial records, STRs.
This builds the proceeds-of-crime picture that CAB acts upon.
Old-craft parallel - the member who notices the unemployed person driving a new car and asks how; the modern
version is structured lifestyle analysis.

6.4 The Criminal Assets Bureau (CAB)
Multi-agency - Gardai, Revenue, Social Welfare, and legal expertise in one body.
Proceeds of Crime Act 1996 / 2005 - civil process to freeze and seize assets shown on the balance of
probabilities to be proceeds of crime.
Does not require a criminal conviction - the civil standard and the proceeds focus are the distinctive power.
Revenue and Social Welfare powers - tax assessment and benefit recovery as parallel levers.
Referral - local intelligence on unexplained wealth can be referred to CAB; the local member's profile feeds
the national capability.
Asset Profilers - trained Divisional members who identify and refer proceeds-of-crime cases to CAB.

6.5 Money Laundering as Intelligence

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Money laundering - placement, layering, integration of criminal proceeds.
Recognising laundering typologies - cash-intensive businesses, structuring, money mules, trade-based
laundering, virtual assets.
Money mules - often vulnerable people recruited to move money; both a target and an intelligence lead to those
above them.
Virtual assets / cryptocurrency - a growing laundering channel; specialist (GNECB / GCCB) capability for
tracing.

6.6 Financial Intelligence in the Inner-City Context
Cash-intensive street crime generates cash that must be laundered - the local economy shows the traces.
Visible proceeds - vehicles, clothing, lifestyle - are intelligence and potential CAB referrals.
Money mules recruited locally - vulnerable young people; safeguarding and intelligence both engage.
The local member's observations of unexplained wealth are the entry point to the financial intelligence system.

6.7 From Financial Intelligence to Action
CAB referral - proceeds-of-crime civil action.
Revenue / Social Welfare action - tax and benefit levers.
Money laundering prosecution - criminal charge under the 2010 Act.
Intelligence development - financial picture corroborates and extends the wider intelligence picture.
Disruption - financial pressure as a lawful disruption tool against organised crime.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 7 - Forensic and Database Intelligence
Forensic and database intelligence turns physical traces and recorded data into links - connecting a person to a
scene, a scene to a scene, a weapon to a series. The DNA database, the fingerprint system, ballistics linkage,
and the Garda information systems (PULSE, GISS) are the modern engines of this discipline. Where HUMINT
tells you who, forensic intelligence often proves it independently - and links cases no human source could
connect.

7.1 The DNA Database System
The Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence and DNA Database System) Act 2014 established the DNA Database
System, operated by Forensic Science Ireland (FSI). It is both an evidential tool and a powerful intelligence
engine.
Reference index - DNA profiles of persons (taken under the Act's powers).
Crime scene index - profiles from crime scene samples.
Matching - scene-to-person matches (identifying a suspect) and scene-to-scene matches (linking offences into
a series).
Intelligence value - a scene-to-scene match links crimes that no other method would connect, revealing a series
or an offender's range.
Powers - the Act governs when samples may be taken, from whom, and retention/destruction.
Familial searching - in limited, governed circumstances, near-matches can indicate a relative; tightly controlled.

7.2 Fingerprints - AFIS
Automated Fingerprint Identification System - searches marks against the fingerprint collection.
Scene marks identified to persons; latent marks linking scenes.
Long-established but continually modernised - the original biometric intelligence tool.
Continuity and expert evidence - the identification is given in evidence by a fingerprint expert.

7.3 Ballistics Linkage
Garda Ballistics Section / FSI - examination of firearms, cartridge cases, projectiles.
Linkage - a fired cartridge case can be linked to a specific firearm; a firearm to multiple incidents.
Intelligence value - linking shootings reveals that one weapon (and likely one network) is responsible for a
series.
Critical in organised / gangland firearms intelligence.

7.4 Other Forensic Intelligence
Drug profiling / analysis - FSI analysis can indicate source, purity, batch links (Vol 5).
Toolmark and trace - linking implements and materials across scenes.
Digital forensics - device extraction as both forensic and technical intelligence (Chapter 5).
Document examination - in fraud and forgery.

7.5 The Garda Information Systems

records, MO data

7.5.1 PULSE as an Intelligence Tool
Person records - history, associations, markers, intelligence entries.
Association mapping - who is linked to whom across incidents.
Vehicle and location links - connecting people, cars, addresses, events.
MO (modus operandi) search - finding offences with matching method.
Intelligence records - graded intelligence stored and retrievable (the modern collator).
The integrity discipline - PULSE entries must be accurate and access must be for a policing purpose (the systemabuse trip-wire, Ethics).

7.6 The Collator - Then and Now
The collator's office - the member (or civilian) who maintained the card index of local criminals, associations,
vehicles, and incidents - was for generations the heart of local intelligence. The modern collator is PULSE and
the local intelligence unit. The function is identical: capture, organise, link, retrieve. The lesson endures intelligence that is not recorded and retrievable is lost. The new systems scale the old craft; they do not replace
the discipline of recording.

7.7 Linkage - The Power of Connection
The distinctive value of forensic and database intelligence is linkage - connecting what otherwise looks
unconnected:
Scene-to-person - identifying who was there.
Scene-to-scene - revealing a series.
Weapon-to-incidents - one firearm across multiple shootings.
Person-to-person - association networks.
Vehicle-to-events - placing a car at scenes.
Each link is intelligence; combined, they build the network and series picture that drives major investigations.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 8 - Building, Analysing and Disseminating
Intelligence
Collection is only the beginning. Raw intelligence has value only when it is built upon, analysed for meaning,
and delivered to those who can act - all while protecting sources and methods and preserving the line between
intelligence and evidence. This chapter covers how fragments become a picture, how the picture becomes
action, and how the intelligence-evidence divide is managed.

8.1 Building on Intelligence - From Fragment to Picture
A single report rarely justifies major action. Intelligence is built by accumulation and corroboration:

8.2 Corroboration - The Central Discipline
Single-source intelligence is fragile - one source can be wrong, deceived, or malicious.
Corroboration from an independent source/method dramatically increases reliability.
Never act on uncorroborated single-source intelligence where the stakes are high (armed operation, dwelling
entry, arrest).
Corroboration also protects the source - if a second, independent line supports the picture, action can rest on
the independent line, shielding the original source (8.5).

8.3 Analysis and Analytical Products
Analysis turns collated intelligence into meaning and decision-support. Trained crime and intelligence analysts
produce structured products:
Target Profile - a comprehensive picture of an individual or group: associations, methods, assets, patterns,
vulnerabilities.
Network / link chart - a visual map of associations and relationships (the modern version of pins and string).
Crime-pattern analysis - identifying series, hotspots, temporal patterns.
Problem profile - deep analysis of a specific recurring problem.
Comparative case analysis - linking offences by method and signature.
Strategic / tactical assessments - the big picture and the now picture (Chapter 1).

8.3.1 Analytical Techniques
Association analysis - mapping who connects to whom and how.
Temporal analysis - when things happen; sequencing events.
Geographic / spatial analysis - where; hotspot mapping; journey-to-crime.
Frequency / trend analysis - what is increasing, clustering, changing.
Inference development - the analyst's reasoned conclusions, flagged as inference not fact.

8.4 Dissemination - Getting It to the Right People
Need-to-know - only those who need the intelligence to act receive it.
Handling code governs - the dissemination grade controls how far it travels (Chapter 2).
Forms of dissemination - briefings (parade, operational), intelligence reports, tasking documents, threat-to-life
warnings, alerts/markers on systems.
Timeliness - intelligence has a shelf life; late dissemination is wasted intelligence.
Feedback - those who receive and act on intelligence should feed back results, closing the cycle.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

8.5 The Intelligence-Evidence Divide and the Sterile Corridor
This is the most important operational discipline in intelligence work. Intelligence and evidence are different
things, and much intelligence can never become evidence without unacceptable cost (revealing a source,
exposing a method). The sterile corridor is the technique that resolves this:

8.5.1 The Problem
Intelligence identifies (e.g.) a drugs stash at a premises - but the intelligence is a graded source report that
cannot be disclosed without endangering the source.
A warrant requires sworn information showing reasonable grounds - which is disclosable.
Putting the source's report directly into the warrant application risks revealing them in the prosecution.

8.5.2 The Solution - The Sterile Corridor
Use the (protected) intelligence to direct independent, evidentially-clean collection.
Example - intelligence indicates the stash; surveillance (independently authorised) observes activity consistent
with dealing; the warrant rests on the surveillance and observation, not the source report.
The independent evidential line is the 'sterile corridor' - it leads to the same place without exposing the original
intelligence.
Result - the prosecution rests on disclosable evidence; the source and method stay protected.

8.5.3 Why This Matters
Protects sources' lives and the ability to recruit future sources.
Protects sensitive methods from disclosure.
Keeps the prosecution clean and disclosable - avoiding the PII confrontation where possible.
Failure to build the corridor - relying on protected intelligence as if it were evidence - collapses cases and
endangers sources (the intelligence-evidence trip-wire, Ethics).

8.6 Public Interest Immunity and Source Protection
Where protected intelligence must be referenced (e.g. to explain the basis of action), Public Interest Immunity
(PII) is the mechanism (Vol 9 Ch 7).
Informer privilege - the identity of a source is protected; the courts recognise a strong (though not absolute)
privilege (the Ward / informer-privilege line).
The 'innocence at stake' exception - source protection yields where disclosure is genuinely necessary to avoid
a miscarriage of justice.
Never disclose a source's identity without consulting the prosecutor / DPP and the source-handling structure.

8.7 Intelligence and the Threat to Life
Intelligence frequently reveals a threat to a person's life (the planned shooting, the contract, the feud target).
This engages an immediate duty:
The Osman / protective obligation - where Gardai know or ought to know of a real and immediate threat to an
identified person's life, operational steps to protect engage (Vol 4 / Vol 8 case law).
The threat-to-life ('GIM' / formal warning) - the person at risk is warned, in a managed way that protects the
source of the intelligence.
Protective measures - varying from advice through to protection operations.
Source protection within the warning - the warning is given without revealing how the threat became known.
This is intelligence at its most consequential - lives turn on getting it right and acting promptly.

8.8 Disruption - Lawful Action Short of Prosecution
Not all intelligence leads to prosecution. Lawful disruption uses intelligence to frustrate criminality by other

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

means:
Overt presence / pressure - making an area or operation untenable for offenders.
Targeted lawful enforcement - using every lawful power (road traffic, warrants, regulatory) against a target.
Financial disruption - CAB referral, asset action (Chapter 6).
Partner action - licensing, regulatory, local authority levers.
Disruption is a legitimate intelligence outcome where prosecution is not (yet) achievable - but it must remain
lawful and proportionate, not harassment.

8.9 The Complete Picture - Intelligence in the Round
The intelligence discipline in one view
Collect lawfully from all source types. Grade honestly - source and information separately, with a handling code.
Record so it is retrievable. Build by corroboration; never act on fragile single-source where the stakes are high.
Analyse for linkage and meaning. Disseminate on need-to-know within the handling code, in time to matter.
Protect sources and methods absolutely within the law - build the sterile corridor so the evidence is clean and the
source is safe. Act on threat-to-life immediately. And remember throughout that intelligence concentrates power
and risk: the integrity, the legality, and the welfare disciplines are not constraints on the work - they are what
make it legitimate.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Practice and the Intelligence File
Part A - Recording Intelligence
Every meaningful report recorded on the intelligence system, however informal the source encounter.
Graded at recording - source / information / handling (Ch 2).
Basis recorded - the source's access, corroboration, reason to credit/doubt.
Linked - connected to existing person / vehicle / location / event records.
Sanitised where needed - describe the information without revealing the source's identity.
Contemporaneous - recorded promptly, not reconstructed later.

Part B - The Source File / Investigation File Separation
Source (CHIS) records kept in a separate, secure source file.
Restricted access - handling team and authorising structure only.
Intelligence enters the investigation file in sanitised, graded form.
This separation is what allows the source to be protected through any prosecution.

Part C - Intelligence in the Investigation File
Intelligence that grounds action must be traceable to lawful, recordable grounds.
Where the grounds are protected intelligence, build the sterile corridor (Ch 8) so the file rests on disclosable
evidence.
Disclosure analysis - identify any intelligence material that may be disclosable; flag sensitive material early for
PII analysis (Vol 9 Ch 7).
Never put a source at risk through careless inclusion of identifying detail in the investigation file.

Part D - Disclosure of Intelligence Material
The continuous disclosure obligation applies (Vol 9 Ch 7).
Sensitive intelligence material - sources, methods, ongoing operations - flagged for PII consideration.
The court conducts the PII balancing; the prosecutor / DPP manages the process.
Informer privilege protects source identity, subject to the innocence-at-stake limit (Ward).
Engage the prosecutor early where intelligence material may become a disclosure issue.

Part E - Authorisation Records
Surveillance authorisations / approvals (2009 Act) - documented, with necessity and proportionality articulated.
Communications data requests - documented within the statutory framework.
CHIS authorisations and tasking - recorded in the source-handling system.
These records both discipline the activity and, where needed, demonstrate its lawfulness.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Evidence and Admissibility (Intelligence)
Part A - The Intelligence-Evidence Divide
Intelligence guides action; evidence proves the case.
Much intelligence cannot be evidence - hearsay, source-protective, method-protective.
The sterile corridor converts an intelligence lead into independent admissible evidence (Ch 8).

Part B - Surveillance Evidence
Product of authorised surveillance (2009 Act) is admissible where the authorisation was valid.
The surveillance log - contemporaneous, accurate - is the evidential record.
Continuity of any recordings / exhibits (Vol 9 PEMS).
The authorising documentation supports admissibility.

Part C - Communications Data Evidence
Data obtained under the proper authorised framework (post-Dwyer) is admissible.
Cell-site analysis - expert evidence; the analyst explains the geographic inference.
Chain from provider to court documented.

Part D - Forensic / Database Evidence
DNA / fingerprint / ballistics matches given in evidence by the relevant expert.
Database match is intelligence (a lead); the confirmatory evidential comparison is the evidence.
Continuity and the statutory sampling framework (2014 DNA Act) underpin admissibility.

Part E - OSINT Evidence
Open-source material captured properly (date/time/URL, preserved) can be evidence.
Attribution must be proven - that the account/content is genuinely the person's.
The member who captured it gives evidence of the capture and continuity.

Part F - Intelligence That Stays Intelligence
Source reports, sensitive methods, and ongoing-operation material typically remain intelligence.
Used to direct the investigation, not to prove the case.
Protected by PII / informer privilege where reference is unavoidable.
The skilled member ensures the case never depends on disclosing what must stay secret.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Intelligence)
Precise framework references for the principal intelligence-related provisions. Verify current text before
relying.

Part A - Surveillance
Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 - covert surveillance authorisation; senior-officer approval (urgent);
tracking devices; admissibility; oversight by designated High Court judge.

Part B - Communications Data
Communications (Retention of Data) Act 2011 - original framework.
Communications (Retention of Data) (Amendment) Act 2022 - post-CJEU/Dwyer reform; targeted retention;
authorised access.
Interception of Postal Packets and Telecommunications Messages (Regulation) Act 1993 - content interception
framework (exceptional).

Part C - Forensic Databases
Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence and DNA Database System) Act 2014 - DNA Database System; sampling
powers; retention/destruction; FSI operation.
Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act 1990 (as amended) - earlier forensic sampling framework.

Part D - Financial Intelligence
Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010 - STR obligations; FIU; designated
persons.
Proceeds of Crime Act 1996 / 2005 - CAB civil proceeds-of-crime process.
Criminal Assets Bureau Act 1996 - establishment and powers of CAB.

Part E - Organised Crime / Serious Crime
Criminal Justice Act 2006 - Part 7 organised crime offences.
Criminal Justice (Amendment) Act 2009 - further organised crime provisions; Special Criminal Court.
Offences Against the State Acts - scheduled offences; s.30 detention (Vol 9).

Part F - Data Protection in Law Enforcement
Data Protection Act 2018 - Part 5 (Law Enforcement Directive as implemented) governs processing of personal
data for law-enforcement purposes.
Policing purpose, necessity, proportionality, and access control govern intelligence-system use.

Part G - Governance / Foundational
Garda Síochána Act 2005 - functions, including security and intelligence functions; accountability framework.
Garda Síochána policy framework on the use of Covert Human Intelligence Sources (internal operational
policy).

Part H - Constitutional / Convention

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Constitution - Article 40.3 (unenumerated right to privacy); Article 40.5 (dwelling); Article 38.1 (fair trial).
ECHR - Article 8 (privacy, engaged by surveillance and data); Article 6 (fair trial, engaged by disclosure/PII);
Article 2 (life, engaged by threat-to-life intelligence).
EU Charter - Articles 7 and 8 (privacy and data protection - the communications-data line).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Case Law Resource (Vol 10 - Criminal
Intelligence)
Intelligence engages a distinctive jurisprudence: the surveillance and covert-powers authorities; the
communications-data line (Digital Rights Ireland through Dwyer, treated in depth in Vol 9 and summarised
here for the intelligence context); the source-protection / informer-privilege and PII line (Ward and the
innocence-at-stake exception); the warrant-independence authority (Damache); and the ECtHR jurisprudence
on surveillance, interception, and source protection (Klass, Malone, Kennedy, Big Brother Watch; Goodwin on
journalistic sources by analogy). This appendix treats the principal authorities in vLex-style depth.
Source verification reminder
Surveillance, communications-data, and source-protection law are fast-moving and heavily litigated, in the Irish
courts, the ECtHR, and the CJEU. Some authorities here are working references requiring exact neutral-citation
verification on BAILII, courts.ie, HUDOC, or curia.europa.eu. The Dwyer line in particular continues to develop.
Verify before relying in any application, statement, or court.

Part A - Surveillance and Covert Powers
A.1 The Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 - Statutory Framework
Framework: The 2009 Act provides the statutory basis for covert surveillance by An Garda Siochana, the
Defence Forces, and Revenue. It establishes a judicial authorisation regime (District Court judge), a seniorofficer approval regime for urgent cases, and a distinct regime for tracking devices.
Principle: Covert surveillance engaging a reasonable expectation of privacy requires authorisation grounded
in necessity (preventing/detecting arrestable offences, State security, saving life) and proportionality. Evidence
obtained under a valid authorisation is admissible.
Significance: Before the 2009 Act, covert surveillance product faced admissibility uncertainty. The Act created
the framework that both disciplines the activity and makes the product usable. Oversight is by a designated
High Court judge, with a complaints mechanism.
Operational Application: Any covert, systematic surveillance must be brought within the Act - authorisation
(or urgent approval) before commencement, necessity and proportionality articulated and recorded, collateral
intrusion minimised. Drifting from routine observation into directed/intrusive surveillance without authority
risks both exclusion of the product and a finding of unlawful conduct.

A.2 ECtHR - Klass v Germany (1978) 2 EHRR 214
Citation: Application No. 5029/71, ECtHR, 6 September 1978.
Facts: German legislation permitting secret surveillance of communications was challenged under Article 8
ECHR.
Holding: Secret surveillance is compatible with Article 8 only where it is in accordance with law, pursues a
legitimate aim, and is necessary in a democratic society - which requires adequate and effective safeguards
against abuse.
Significance: Foundational ECtHR authority on secret surveillance. Established that covert powers, while
permissible, demand safeguards - clear legal basis, oversight, and limits - because of their inherent capacity for
abuse.
Operational Application: The Klass principles underpin the Irish 2009 Act's safeguard architecture. For the
member, the lesson is that covert power is conditional on the safeguards - authorisation, necessity,
proportionality, oversight - being genuinely observed, not merely formally present.

A.3 ECtHR - Malone v UK (1984) 7 EHRR 14
Citation: Application No. 8691/79, ECtHR, 2 August 1984.
Facts: Interception of communications and metering of telephone calls in the UK without a clear statutory basis

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

was challenged.
Holding: Violation of Article 8. The interception was not 'in accordance with the law' because the legal basis
was insufficiently clear and accessible. Notably, the Court treated communications metering data (the numbers
called - what we now call communications data) as engaging Article 8, distinct from content but still protected.
Significance: Established the 'in accordance with law' requirement - covert powers need a clear, accessible,
foreseeable legal basis - and that communications data, not just content, engages privacy. This distinction runs
through to the modern Dwyer line.
Operational Application: Communications data access is a privacy intrusion requiring proper legal basis (the
Retention of Data framework, post-Dwyer). Malone is why metadata is regulated, not free.

A.4 ECtHR - Kennedy v UK (2010) 52 EHRR 4
Citation: Application No. 26839/05, ECtHR, 18 May 2010.
Facts: Challenge to the UK interception regime and its oversight.
Holding: The regime, with its safeguards and oversight (including an independent tribunal), was compatible
with Article 8. The Court restated the quality-of-law and safeguard requirements for secret surveillance.
Significance: Modern restatement of the Klass safeguards in a developed statutory context - clarity of law,
limits on duration and scope, oversight, and remedy.
Operational Application: Confirms that a properly-safeguarded and overseen covert regime is Conventioncompliant; the safeguards are what carry it. Reinforces the discipline around the Irish 2009 Act and commsdata regimes.

A.5 ECtHR - Big Brother Watch v UK (2021) [GC]
Citation: Applications Nos. 58170/13, 62322/14, 24960/15, Grand Chamber, 25 May 2021.
Facts: Challenge to bulk interception and intelligence-sharing regimes following the Snowden disclosures.
Holding: Bulk interception is not inherently incompatible with Article 8, but requires 'end-to-end safeguards'
- including independent authorisation, defined scope, and oversight at each stage. Deficiencies in the UK regime
were found.
Significance: The leading modern authority on bulk and large-scale surveillance. Establishes that scale
intensifies, rather than removes, the safeguard requirement.
Operational Application: Large-scale data and surveillance methods demand proportionate, well-governed,
overseen handling. For the member, the principle scales down: the more powerful and broad the method, the
tighter the justification and safeguards must be.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part B - Communications Data (Intelligence Context)
The CJEU communications-data line is treated in full vLex depth in Volume 9 (Digital Rights Ireland;
Tele2/Watson; La Quadrature du Net; Commissioner of An Garda Siochana v Dwyer; Privacy International).
For the intelligence context, the essential points:

B.1 Commissioner of An Garda Siochana v Dwyer (Case C-140/20, CJEU 2022) - The
Irish Pivot
Significance (intelligence): Confirmed that general and indiscriminate retention of communications data is
incompatible with EU law, driving the Communications (Retention of Data) (Amendment) Act 2022. For
intelligence, the consequence is operational: access to retained communications data must now be targeted,
necessary, proportionate, and independently authorised. Speculative or broad trawling is no longer compatible.
Operational Application: When seeking communications data for an intelligence purpose, the request must
be specific to an investigation, justified by necessity and proportionality, and processed through the proper
authorisation route. The cell-site and traffic-data product remains available - but only within the disciplined
framework.

B.2 The Retention vs Access Distinction
The CJEU line distinguishes retention (the holding of data, which must be targeted/limited) from access (which
must be independently authorised and limited to serious crime / equivalent). For the member, the operational
touchpoint is access - obtaining the data through the lawful, authorised route. The framework continues to
evolve; verify the current position.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part C - Source Protection, Informer Privilege and PII
C.1 DPP v Special Criminal Court and Ward [1999] 1 IR 60 - Informer Privilege / PII
Citation: [1999] 1 IR 60 (Supreme Court).
Facts: The case concerned disclosure of material in the possession of the prosecution and the scope of public
interest privilege, in the context of the Special Criminal Court. The interplay of the accused's right to a fair trial
and the public interest in protecting sensitive material (including informer identity) was central.
Holding: There is a strong public interest in protecting sensitive material, including the identity of informers,
through public interest privilege / immunity. However, this is not absolute - it yields where disclosure is
genuinely necessary to avoid a real risk of an unfair trial (the 'innocence at stake' principle). The court conducts
the balancing, and may inspect the material.
Significance: Leading Irish authority on PII and informer privilege in the criminal context. Establishes both
the strength of source protection and its limit - the privilege protects the source, but not at the cost of convicting
the innocent.
Operational Application: Source identity is protected by a strong privilege - but the member must understand
it is not guaranteed in every circumstance. This is precisely why the sterile corridor (Ch 8) matters: building an
independent evidential case means the protected intelligence rarely needs to be disclosed, avoiding the PII
confrontation altogether. Never disclose a source without DPP / prosecutor and source-handling consultation;
never assume protection is automatic if innocence is genuinely at stake.

C.2 The Informer Privilege Principle - General
Principle: The common law recognises a privilege protecting the identity of police informers, rooted in the
public interest in maintaining the flow of information and protecting sources from reprisal.
Limit: The privilege yields where the disclosure of the informer's identity is necessary to establish the accused's
innocence (the 'innocence at stake' exception).
Operational Application: The privilege is the legal expression of the source-protection duty. The member's
handling discipline (separate source file, sanitisation, sterile corridor) is what makes the privilege effective in
practice and rarely tested.

C.3 ECtHR - Goodwin v UK (1996) 22 EHRR 123 (by analogy - source protection)
Citation: Application No. 17488/90, ECtHR, 27 March 1996.
Significance: Though concerning journalistic source protection under Article 10, Goodwin establishes the
broader principle that the protection of confidential sources is a value of high importance, interference with
which requires an overriding requirement in the public interest. By analogy it illuminates why source protection
in the policing context carries such weight - and why it, too, is strong but not limitless.
Operational Application: Reinforces the seriousness of the source-protection duty as a recognised value,
while situating it within a balancing framework.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part D - Warrants, Entrapment and the Intelligence-Action Interface
D.1 Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11 - Warrant Independence
Citation: [2012] IESC 11 (Supreme Court).
Significance (intelligence): Damache held that a search warrant issued by a member of the investigating force
breached the separation between investigation and the independent authorisation of intrusion. For intelligence
work, the lesson is structural: the move from intelligence to coercive action (the warrant) must pass through an
independent authoriser. Intelligence justifies seeking the warrant; an independent judicial officer authorises it.
Operational Application: When intelligence grounds a warrant application, the sworn information must show
genuine reasonable grounds (built via the sterile corridor where the underlying intelligence is protected), and
the warrant must come from the proper independent issuer. Treated in full in Vol 2 and Vol 5 case law.

D.2 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 - Suspicion Must Be the Member's Own
Significance (intelligence): Walsh holds that the arresting member must personally hold reasonable suspicion.
For intelligence work, this is the bridge between intelligence and arrest: intelligence can inform and contribute
to the member's suspicion, but the member must genuinely form their own suspicion on grounds they are aware
of. 'Intelligence said so' is not, by itself, the member's own suspicion - the member must be told the substance
and form their own view.
Operational Application: Acting on intelligence at the point of arrest requires the member to have the
substance of the grounds and to form genuine personal suspicion. Treated in full in Vol 2 case law.

D.3 The Entrapment / Agent Provocateur Limit
Principle: Evidence obtained where law enforcement (or a source acting on their behalf) incited, instigated, or
procured an offence the accused would not otherwise have committed is liable to be excluded, and the conduct
is improper. The source may observe and report on planned crime; the source must not cause it.
Operational Application: A critical limit on CHIS tasking (Ch 3). Tasking must never cross from gathering
intelligence on existing/intended criminality into inciting it. The line protects both the integrity of any resulting
case and the propriety of the operation.

D.4 DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 - The Exclusionary Rule in the Intelligence Context
Significance (intelligence): JC restated the exclusionary rule - unconstitutionally obtained evidence may be
admitted where the breach was inadvertent, but deliberate or reckless breach leads to exclusion. For
intelligence-led operations, the lesson is that procedural and constitutional rigour at the action stage (warrant,
surveillance authorisation, arrest) is what protects the resulting evidence. Cutting corners because 'the
intelligence was good' risks exclusion.
Operational Application: Good intelligence does not excuse a bad procedure. The action that flows from
intelligence must itself be constitutionally clean. Treated in full in Vol 2 case law.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part E - Key Operational Takeaways from Case Law
E.1 Covert Power Is Conditional on Safeguards
Klass through Big Brother Watch: covert and large-scale methods are permissible only with genuine safeguards
- legal basis, authorisation, necessity, proportionality, oversight. The Irish 2009 Act embodies this. The
safeguards are the licence.

E.2 Communications Data Is Regulated, Not Free
Malone through Dwyer: metadata engages privacy; access must be targeted, necessary, proportionate, and
authorised. The 2022 framework is the operating reality.

E.3 Source Protection Is Strong but Not Absolute
Ward and informer privilege: the source is protected by a strong privilege that yields only where innocence is
genuinely at stake. The sterile corridor is how the member keeps the privilege from ever being tested.

E.4 The Move from Intelligence to Action Must Be Clean
Damache, Walsh, JC: the warrant needs an independent issuer and genuine grounds; the arrest needs the
member's own suspicion; the procedure must be constitutionally clean. Good intelligence never excuses a
defective action.

E.5 The Source Must Not Cause the Crime
The entrapment limit: tasking gathers intelligence on criminality; it must never incite it. The line protects the
case and the integrity of the work.

E.6 Integrity Throughout
Across every authority runs one theme: intelligence power is legitimate only when exercised with integrity and
within the law. The grading, the authorisation, the source protection, the clean action - these are not obstacles
to effective intelligence work. They are what make it stand up, stay secret where it must, and serve justice rather
than undermine it.

For probationer and experienced member reference
All names, addresses, reference numbers and scenarios in worked examples are fictitious. Any resemblance to real persons or events is coincidental and
not intended.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 11 - A Note on the Material
Volumes 1 through 10 build operational knowledge of offences, procedures, powers and intelligence. Volume
11 is about applying that knowledge through the written and spoken artefacts of investigation: the cover report
that travels to the DPP, the statement that becomes evidence, the message that keeps the injured party informed,
and the cross-examination that tests it all in court. The work is judged on these outputs more than on anything
else. A clean arrest can be lost to a sloppy cover report; a strong case can collapse on a poorly-prepared crossexamination. This volume covers the craft of communicating an investigation - through the discipline of the
theme, the architecture of the document, and the preparation that survives the witness box.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

How to Use Volume 11
Volume 11 is organised in five parts plus appendices:
Part A - The Theme Doctrine (Chapter 1). The central organising principle for every report, statement and case
presentation.
Part B - Cover Reports (Chapters 2 to 4). Architecture, types and fully worked fictitious examples.
Part C - Statements (Chapters 5 to 7). Taking statements from witnesses and injured parties, drafting the Garda's
own statement, fully worked fictitious examples.
Part D - Investigation Closure and Injured Party Communications (Chapter 8). Templates and the Victims
Charter framework.
Part E - Defence Preparation and Cross-Examination (Chapters 9 to 12). Case law attack vectors, crossexamination roadmaps by offence, and the witness-box survival framework.
Appendices - Quick-reference templates and standard elements.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The Five Volume 11 Mnemonics
THEME - Identifying the Theme
T - Threshold - what does the prosecution have to prove.
H - Heart - what is the heart of the case (the single most compelling thread).
E - Evidence - what evidence supports the heart.
M - Motive - why did the suspect act (context not always essential but always helpful).
E - Exposure - where is the weakness; what will the defence attack.

COVER - The Cover Report Architecture
C - Charge clearly stated.
O - Outline of the offence and how it engages the elements.
V - Voice of the evidence - statements, exhibits, technical material listed.
E - Examination - analysis showing each element is met.
R - Recommendation to the prosecutor.

STORY - The Statement Architecture
S - Setting - where, when, who was the speaker, in what capacity.
T - Trigger - what brought the speaker into contact with the events.
O - Observation - what was seen, heard, said, done; first-person, sensory, contemporaneous.
R - Result - what flowed from the observation; arrest, exhibit seized, statement taken.
Y - Yield to verification - the signature, the date, the contemporaneous record.

CLOSURE - Injured Party Communication
C - Contact maintained throughout.
L - Loop closed - the final outcome communicated.
O - Options - the IP's rights and options explained.
S - Support service signposted where appropriate.
U - Understand the IP's perspective.
R - Record the communication on PULSE / file.
E - Empathy - the tone matters.

CROSS - Preparing for Cross-Examination
C - Case law - which authorities are likely to be raised against you.
R - Read - re-read your statement, your notebook, the custody record, before going into court.
O - Own grounds - know the specific basis of every decision (arrest, search, caution).
S - Slow - take the time to answer; never rush.
S - Stick to the truth - including admitting limits of recollection.
Verification reminder
The templates, worked examples and cross-examination roadmaps in this volume are operational guidance, not
legal authority. Statute, case law and Garda HQ policy continue to develop. Verify current statutory text, case
law and DPP/Garda procedural directions before relying. The cross-examination Q&A roadmaps anticipate likely
defence patterns but are not exhaustive - every contested case is unique. Use the framework, not the script.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Code of Ethics - Practical Application
The artefacts of investigation - cover reports, statements, IP communications, court testimony - are where
integrity is most visible and most consequential. The Code engages with particular force here, because each
document carries a Garda's signature and stands as evidence of the work.

Standard 2 - Honesty and Integrity
Every line in a cover report and every paragraph in a statement is a representation of fact. Exaggeration,
omission, embellishment or invention - even at the margins - are integrity failures. The discipline is to write
only what is supported by the contemporaneous record. The statement that survives cross-examination is the
statement that was true when it was written.

Standard 3 - Accountability
Reports and statements are how the Garda's work is accountable to the prosecutor, the court and the public.
They must be capable of being scrutinised - by the DPP on receipt, by the defence on disclosure, by the trial
judge in ruling, by appellate courts on review. Build for that scrutiny from the first draft.

Standard 5 - Respect
How an injured party is communicated with matters at least as much as the investigative outcome. A successful
prosecution that leaves the IP unheard and unsupported is a partial failure. The CLOSURE mnemonic is
operational ethics expressed as a workflow.

Standard 6 - Confidentiality
Statements and reports contain sensitive material. Disclosure within the lawful framework is the norm; sharing
outside that framework - even with colleagues without need to know - is a breach. IP communications, in
particular, must avoid disclosing material that should not yet be shared (suspect identity before charge, thirdparty private information, sensitive intelligence).

Five Practical Application Trip-Wires
